session_metadata.subcategory: Proof-Based Mathematics — Abstract Algebra, Advanced Linear Algebra

tasks.prompt: Let A be a real 4x4 matrix satisfying A^T = A, A^2 = I_4, and tr(A) = 0. Prove that there exists an orthogonal matrix Q such that Q^T A Q = diag(1,1,-1,-1). Your proof must explicitly justify every eigenvalue claim and multiplicity argument.
constraints: Use the real spectral theorem explicitly
Prove eigenvalues are only +1 and -1 from A^2 = I_4
Use trace to determine multiplicities

solution_steps: Step 1: Since A is real symmetric, by the spectral theorem there exists orthogonal Q with Q^T A Q diagonal having real eigenvalues lambda_1,...,lambda_4.
Step 2: From A^2 = I_4, every eigenvalue lambda satisfies lambda^2 = 1, hence lambda in {1,-1}.
Step 3: Let m_+ and m_- be multiplicities of 1 and -1. Then m_+ + m_- = 4.
Step 4: The trace equals sum of eigenvalues: tr(A) = m_+ - m_- = 0.
Step 5: Solve the system m_+ + m_- = 4 and m_+ - m_- = 0 to get m_+ = m_- = 2.
Step 6: Therefore the diagonal form is exactly diag(1,1,-1,-1) up to permutation of diagonal entries.
Step 7: Permuting orthonormal eigenbasis vectors if needed gives the stated diagonal matrix.

common_errors: Using diagonalizability over C without proving orthogonal diagonalization over R
Forgetting that A^2=I forces minimal polynomial dividing x^2-1
Using determinant instead of trace to determine both multiplicities
required_knowledge: Spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices

tasks.subcategory: Galois Theory — Splitting Field of x^4-2
tasks.prompt: Let f(x)=x^4-2 in Q[x]. Determine the splitting field L over Q and prove that Gal(L/Q) is isomorphic to the dihedral group D4 of order 8. Your proof must include: (i) [L:Q], (ii) explicit generators of Gal(L/Q), and (iii) relations proving the group structure.
constraints: Do not cite a classification table of quartic Galois groups
You must prove irreducibility of x^4-2 over Q
You must write explicit automorphisms on generators

solution_steps: Step 1: f(x)=x^4-2 is irreducible over Q by Eisenstein at p=2.
Step 2: Let alpha = 2^(1/4). Roots are alpha, -alpha, i alpha, -i alpha. So splitting field is L=Q(alpha,i).
Step 3: [Q(alpha):Q]=4. Since Q(alpha) subset R, i notin Q(alpha), so [Q(alpha,i):Q(alpha)]=2. Hence [L:Q]=8.
Step 4: Define sigma by sigma(alpha)=i alpha, sigma(i)=i. Define tau by tau(alpha)=alpha, tau(i)=-i. Both extend uniquely to Q-automorphisms of L.
Step 5: sigma has order 4 (alpha -> i alpha -> -alpha -> -i alpha -> alpha). tau has order 2.
Step 6: Compute conjugation relation on generators: tau sigma tau(alpha)=tau sigma(alpha)=tau(i alpha)=-i alpha=sigma^{-1}(alpha), and tau sigma tau(i)=i=sigma^{-1}(i). So tau sigma tau = sigma^{-1}.
Step 7: Group generated by sigma,tau has defining relations sigma^4=tau^2=1 and tau sigma tau=sigma^{-1}, hence is D4. Its order is 8, matching [L:Q], so Gal(L/Q) ~= D4.

common_errors: Claiming splitting field is Q(alpha) and forgetting imaginary roots
Assuming Gal group is cyclic of order 8 without checking relations
Not proving i is outside Q(alpha)

tasks.subcategory: Linear Algebra — Rank-Nullity on Quotient Spaces
tasks.prompt:
Let V and W be finite-dimensional vector spaces over a field F, and let T:V->W be linear. Let U<=V and Z<=W satisfy T(U) subseteq Z. Define the induced linear map Tbar: V/U -> W/Z by Tbar(v+U)=T(v)+Z.

Prove all of the following:
(1) ker(Tbar) = T^{-1}(Z)/U.
(2) im(Tbar) = (T(V)+Z)/Z.
(3) dim(V/U) = dim(T^{-1}(Z)/U) + dim((T(V)+Z)/Z).

You must justify each equality as an equality of subspaces, not just dimensions.
constraints: You must prove Tbar is well-defined before using it
You must prove both inclusions in each set equality
Use rank-nullity only after identifying kernel and image correctly

solution_steps: Step 1: Well-definedness: if v+U=v'+U then v-v' in U, so T(v)-T(v') in T(U) subseteq Z, hence T(v)+Z=T(v')+Z.
Step 2: Kernel description: v+U in ker(Tbar) iff T(v)+Z=Z iff T(v) in Z iff v in T^{-1}(Z). So ker(Tbar)={v+U: v in T^{-1}(Z)}=T^{-1}(Z)/U.
Step 3: Image description: im(Tbar)={T(v)+Z: v in V}. This equals (T(V)+Z)/Z by definition of quotient image cosets.
Step 4: Now apply rank-nullity to Tbar: dim(V/U)=dim ker(Tbar)+dim im(Tbar).
Step 5: Substitute from Steps 2 and 3 to obtain dim(V/U)=dim(T^{-1}(Z)/U)+dim((T(V)+Z)/Z).

common_errors: Skipping well-definedness and using Tbar immediately
Writing ker(Tbar)=T^{-1}(Z) without quotienting by U
Confusing T(V)/Z with (T(V)+Z)/Z when Z not subseteq T(V)

tasks.subcategory: Ring Theory — PID/UFD Failure in Z[sqrt(-5)]
tasks.prompt:
In R = Z[sqrt(-5)], prove both statements:

(A) R is not a PID by proving the ideal I=(2,1+sqrt(-5)) is not principal.
(B) R is not a UFD by giving two non-associate irreducible factorizations of 6.

You must justify all norm arguments and associate claims explicitly.
constraints: Use N(a+b sqrt(-5)) = a^2+5b^2 and prove multiplicativity when used
For part (A), rule out all possible norms of a principal generator
For part (B), prove irreducibility of each factor used

solution_steps: Step 1: Norm N(a+b sqrt(-5))=a^2+5b^2 is multiplicative and N(x)=1 iff x is a unit, so units are +-1.
Step 2: Suppose I=(alpha). Then alpha divides 2 and 1+sqrt(-5), so N(alpha) divides gcd(N(2),N(1+sqrt(-5)))=gcd(4,6)=2.
Step 3: Thus N(alpha) in {1,2}. N(alpha)=1 makes alpha a unit, so I=R, impossible since 1 notin I. N(alpha)=2 impossible because a^2+5b^2=2 has no integer solution.
Step 4: Contradiction, so I is not principal. Hence R is not a PID.
Step 6: Irreducibility of 2 and 3: norms 4 and 9; nontrivial factorizations would require elements of norm 2 or 3, but equations a^2+5b^2=2 and =3 have no integer solutions.
Step 7: Irreducibility of 1+-sqrt(-5): norm 6; nontrivial factorization would force norm pair (2,3), impossible by Step 6 norm equations.
Step 8: Distinctness up to associates: associates differ by unit +-1 only; 2,3 are not associates to 1+-sqrt(-5), and 1+sqrt(-5) not associate to 1-sqrt(-5).
Step 9: Therefore 6 has two genuinely different irreducible factorizations, so R is not a UFD.

common_errors: Claiming I is nonprincipal without contradiction via norm divisibility
Using irreducible implies prime in this ring without proof
Not verifying that I is proper before excluding unit generators

tasks.subcategory: Error Detection — Homomorphisms and First Isomorphism Theorem
tasks.prompt:
A student claims:

"Define phi: Z x Z -> Z/12Z by phi(a,b)=4a+6b (mod 12). Since phi is surjective, it is injective. Therefore ker(phi) is the cyclic subgroup generated by (3,-2), because 4*3+6*(-2)=0. By the first isomorphism theorem, (Z x Z)/<(3,-2)> ~= Z/12Z."

Find every substantive error and give a corrected full solution: compute Im(phi), compute ker(phi) exactly, and state the correct quotient-isomorphism.
constraints: Identify at least two distinct logical mistakes
Kernel computation must use congruences mod 12 correctly
Final isomorphism must include the correct image group

solution_steps: Step 1: Error 1: Surjective does not imply injective in general; here domain is infinite and codomain finite, so injectivity is impossible.
Step 2: Error 2: The map is not surjective. Image is subgroup generated by 4 and 6 in Z/12Z, i.e. gcd(4,6,12)=2 gives Im(phi)=2Z/12Z={0,2,4,6,8,10}, size 6.
Step 3: Kernel condition: 4a+6b congruent 0 mod 12. Divide by 2: 2a+3b congruent 0 mod 6. Mod 2 implies b even, b=2t. Then 2a+6t congruent 0 mod 6, so a multiple of 3: a=3s.
Step 4: Therefore ker(phi)= {(3s,2t): s,t in Z} = 3Z x 2Z = <(3,0),(0,2)> (not cyclic generated by (3,-2)).
Step 5: First isomorphism theorem gives (Z x Z)/(3Z x 2Z) ~= Im(phi) ~= Z/6Z.

common_errors: Assuming surjective implies injective without finite-equal-cardinality hypothesis
Computing image as whole Z/12Z when gcd of coefficients is not 1
Treating a rank-2 kernel lattice as cyclic
required_knowledge: Image of linear combinations mod n

tasks.subcategory: Error Detection — Sylow Counting and Diagonalizability
tasks.prompt:
Two arguments are proposed. Both are flawed.

Argument A (Sylow):
"If |G|=42, then n_7 divides 7 and n_7 congruent 1 mod 7, so n_7=7. Hence Sylow 7-subgroup is not normal. Also n_3 divides 3 and n_3 congruent 1 mod 3, so n_3=3. Therefore G is simple."

Argument B (linear algebra):
"If A in M_5(C) has characteristic polynomial (x-1)^3 (x+2)^2, then A is diagonalizable because all eigenvalues are in C."

For each argument, identify the exact incorrect line(s), state the corrected theorem/fact, and derive the correct conclusion for the given data.
constraints: For Argument A, list the correct possibilities for n_7 and n_3
For Argument B, state the correct minimal-polynomial criterion
Conclusions must follow from corrected statements

solution_steps: Step 1 (A error): For |G|=42=2*3*7, Sylow gives n_7 divides 42/7=6 (not 7) and n_7 congruent 1 mod 7, hence n_7=1 only.
Step 2 (A consequence): Sylow 7-subgroup is normal. Therefore G is not simple immediately.
Step 3 (A extra): n_3 divides 14 and n_3 congruent 1 mod 3, so n_3 in {1,7}; the student's n_3=3 is invalid.
Step 4 (B error): Having eigenvalues in C is insufficient for diagonalizability because Jordan blocks may be nontrivial.
Step 5 (B corrected fact): A matrix over C is diagonalizable iff its minimal polynomial splits into distinct linear factors.

Step 6 (B corrected conclusion): From characteristic polynomial alone one cannot conclude diagonalizability; both diagonalizable and non-diagonalizable matrices with that characteristic polynomial exist.

common_errors: Using n_p divides p instead of n_p divides |G|/p^a
Concluding simplicity without checking n_p=1 normality criterion
Confusing characteristic polynomial data with diagonalizability

tasks.subcategory: Group Theory — Sylow Constraint via True/False
tasks.prompt:
True or False (justify rigorously):

Claim: Every group of order 42 has a normal subgroup of order 7.

You must prove or disprove the claim using Sylow theorems, with explicit arithmetic constraints on the number of Sylow 7-subgroups.
constraints: Use Sylow congruence and divisibility conditions explicitly
Conclude normality from the exact value of n_7
No classification theorem of groups of order 42 may be used

solution_steps: Step 1: Let G be of order 42=2*3*7. By Sylow, n_7 divides 42/7=6 and n_7 congruent 1 mod 7.
Step 2: Divisors of 6 are 1,2,3,6. Among these, only 1 is congruent to 1 mod 7.
Step 3: Therefore n_7=1.
Step 4: A Sylow p-subgroup is normal iff it is unique. Hence the Sylow 7-subgroup is normal.
Step 5: Its order is 7 by definition of Sylow 7-subgroup. So G has a normal subgroup of order 7.

common_errors: Using wrong divisor set for n_7
Forgetting the congruence condition n_7 congruent 1 mod 7
Concluding existence of order-7 subgroup but not proving normality

tasks.subcategory: Galois Theory — Open-Ended Intermediate Field Classification
tasks.prompt:
Open-ended classification task.

Let L = Q(zeta_8), where zeta_8 = exp(2pi i / 8). Classify all intermediate fields E with Q subseteq E subseteq L. For each E, determine [E:Q] and whether E/Q is Galois.

Your classification must be complete and justified via explicit Galois correspondence.
constraints: You must compute Gal(L/Q) explicitly
You must list all subgroups and corresponding fixed fields
You must justify why the list is complete

solution_steps: Step 1: L=Q(zeta_8)=Q(i,sqrt(2)). Since phi(8)=4, [L:Q]=4 and Gal(L/Q) ~= (Z/8Z)^x={1,3,5,7} ~= C2 x C2.
Step 2: A Klein four group has exactly 5 subgroups: {1}, three subgroups of order 2, and the whole group.
Step 3: By Galois correspondence, there are exactly 5 intermediate fields.
Step 4: The three index-2 subgroups correspond to quadratic fixed fields: Q(i), Q(sqrt(2)), and Q(i sqrt(2))=Q(sqrt(-2)).
Step 5: The trivial subgroup corresponds to L itself, and the whole Galois group corresponds to Q.
Step 6: Therefore complete list is Q, Q(i), Q(sqrt(2)), Q(sqrt(-2)), L.
Step 7: Degrees over Q are 1,2,2,2,4 respectively.

Step 8: Each E/Q is Galois because Gal(L/Q) is abelian, hence every subgroup is normal and each fixed field is normal over Q; alternatively each listed quadratic field is splitting field of x^2+1, x^2-2, or x^2+2.

common_errors: Listing only Q(i) and Q(sqrt(2)) and missing Q(sqrt(-2))
Using subgroup count incorrectly for V4
Claiming non-Galois intermediate fields despite abelian Galois group
